Licensed to the .NET Foundation under one or more agreements.\n// The .NET Foundation licenses this file to you under the MIT license.\n\nusing System.Collections.Concurrent;\nusing System.ComponentModel;\nusing System.ComponentModel.DataAnnotations;\nusing System.Linq.Expressions;\nusing System.Reflection;\nusing Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.HotReload;\n\nnamespace Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.Forms;\n\ninternal static class ExpressionMemberAccessor\n{\n    private static readonly ConcurrentDictionary<Expression, MemberInfo> _memberInfoCache = new();\n    private static readonly ConcurrentDictionary<MemberInfo, string> _displayNameCache = new();\n\n    static ExpressionMemberAccessor()\n    {\n        if (HotReloadManager.Default.MetadataUpdateSupported)\n        {\n            HotReloadManager.Default.OnDeltaApplied += ClearCache;\n        }\n    }\n\n    private static MemberInfo GetMemberInfo<TValue>(Expression<Func<TValue>> accessor)\n    {\n        ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull(accessor);\n\n        return _memberInfoCache.GetOrAdd(accessor, static expr =>\n        {\n            var lambdaExpression = (LambdaExpression)expr;\n            var accessorBody = lambdaExpression.Body;\n\n            if (accessorBody is UnaryExpression unaryExpression\n                && unaryExpression.NodeType == ExpressionType.Convert\n                && unaryExpression.Type == typeof(object))\n            {\n                accessorBody = unaryExpression.Operand;\n            }\n\n            if (accessorBody is not MemberExpression memberExpression)\n            {\n                throw new ArgumentException(\n                    $\"The provided expression contains a {accessorBody.GetType().Name} which is not supported. \" +\n                    $\"Only simple member accessors (fields, properties) of an object are supported.\");\n            }\n\n            return memberExpression.Member;\n        });\n    }\n\n    public static string GetDisplayName(MemberInfo member)\n    {\n        ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull(member);\n\n        return _displayNameCache.GetOrAdd(member, static m =>\n        {\n            var displayNameAttribute = m.GetCustomAttribute<DisplayNameAttribute>();\n            if (displayNameAttribute?.DisplayName is not null)\n            {\n                return displayNameAttribute.DisplayName;\n            }\n\n            var displayAttribute = m.GetCustomAttribute<DisplayAttribute>();\n            if (displayAttribute is not null)\n            {\n                var name = displayAttribute.GetName();\n                if (name is not null)\n                {\n                    return name;\n                }\n            }\n\n            return m.Name;\n        });\n    }\n\n    public static string GetDisplayName<TValue>(Expression<Func<TValue>> accessor)\n    {\n        ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull(accessor);\n        var member = GetMemberInfo(accessor);\n        return GetDisplayName(member);\n    }\n\n    private static void ClearCache()\n    {\n        _memberInfoCache.Clear();\n    }\n}\n","numLines":91,"startLine":1,"totalLines":91}}}

This is a contradiction. However, looking at line 39, we pass `For` to `ExpressionMemberAccessor.GetDisplayName(For)`, which can throw `ArgumentNullException`. The issue here is:\n   - If `For` is null, the component will throw an exception during parameter setting\n   - The parameter should probably not be nullable OR should have null handling\n   - But actually, since it's `EditorRequired`, the compiler expects it to be set, so making it nullable is probably the bug.\n\n2. **Line 41**: After updating `_displayName`, we call `_renderHandle.Render(BuildRenderTree)`. However, the issue is:\n   - We need to check if the display name actually changed before rendering\n   - Currently, we always render even if the display name is the same as before\n   - This violates the compliance requirement to avoid unnecessary re-rendering\n   - We should only render if `_displayName != _previousFieldAccessor` or something similar\n   - Actually, looking more closely, we have `_previousFieldAccessor` field but we never use it to check if the `For` expression changed\n   - **BUG**: We're not caching the previous `For` expression and comparing it. We should only render if the expression changed.\n\n3. **Line 39**: We call `GetDisplayName(For)` directly without checking if `For` is null, even though `For` is nullable. This could throw.\n\n## ExpressionMemberAccessor.cs Analysis\n\n1. **Line 53**: There's a `GetDisplayName(MemberInfo member)` method.\n\n2. **Line 59-63**: We check for `DisplayNameAttribute` first. **CRITICAL BUG** - the condition is:\n   ```csharp\n   if (displayNameAttribute?.DisplayName is not null)\n   ```\n   The null-coalescing operator `?.` returns null if `displayNameAttribute` is null, so the `is not null` check correctly evaluates to false in that case. The logic is sound. Looking at the attribute precedence, the code checks `DisplayNameAttribute` first, then falls back to `DisplayAttribute`. This ordering seems intentional and matches the documented behavior in DisplayName.cs line 12.\n\n   There's a critical issue in `ClearCache()` at line 88—it clears `_memberInfoCache` but leaves `_displayNameCache` untouched. When hot reload occurs and `OnDeltaApplied` triggers `ClearCache()`, any changes to DisplayNameAttribute or DisplayAttribute won't be reflected because the stale cached values persist. This breaks the hot reload functionality.\n\n   The `GetDisplayName` method at lines 79-84 needs examination as well.\n   ```\n\ncsharp\n   public string GetDisplayName<TValue>(Expression<Func<TValue>> accessor)\n   ```\n   It accepts a nullable expression but immediately calls `ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull(accessor)`, which will throw if null is passed. This connects to DisplayName.cs line 27 where `For` is declared as nullable (`Expression<Func<TValue>>?`) yet marked `[EditorRequired]`. When `For` is passed to `GetDisplayName(For)` at line 39, it can throw despite being required. The fix is to remove the `?` from the property type declaration so the nullability contract matches the actual requirements.\n\nThere's also a re-rendering inefficiency: the `_previousFieldAccessor` field exists but is never checked. Currently, `SetParametersAsync` unconditionally calls `_renderHandle.Render(BuildRenderTree)` every time parameters are set, even if `For` hasn't actually changed. In Blazor, rendering should only occur when relevant parameters change, so I need to compare the new `For` value against the stored previous value and only trigger a render if they differ. would be to only render when the display name actually changes. We could store the previous display name and compare it before triggering a render, since that's the only thing we're actually rendering. Alternatively, we could cache the member info from the expression instead of the expression itself, since Expression objects use reference equality and won't reliably detect when the same logical expression is passed in a different form. The simplest fix is probably just checking if `_displayName` differs from its previous value before calling `_renderHandle.Render`.\n\nLooking at whether this is actually a bug: unnecessary re-rendering violates ASP.NET Core component conventions—components should only render when their state meaningfully changes. This is a compliance issue, not just inefficiency.\n\nHere are the bugs I've identified:\n\n## Bug 1: DisplayName.cs - Nullable parameter contradiction\n- **File**: DisplayName.cs\n- **Line**: 27\n- **Severity**: critical\n- **Description**: The `For` parameter is nullable (`?`) yet marked `EditorRequired`, and `GetDisplayName(For)` will throw if null. The nullability annotation contradicts the actual requirements.\n- **Fix**: Remove the `?` from the property declaration\n\n## Bug 2: DisplayName.cs - Redundant rendering on parameter changes\n- **File**: DisplayName.cs\n- **Line**: 35-44\n- **Severity**: medium\n- **Description**: `SetParametersAsync` always triggers a render without verifying the display name actually changed. The unused `_previousFieldAccessor` field suggests this check was intended. The component should only re-render when the output differs.\n- **Fix**: Compare the new display name against the previous value before calling `_renderHandle.Render`\n\n## Bug 3: ExpressionMemberAccessor.cs - Incomplete cache invalidation\n- **File**: ExpressionMemberAccessor.cs\n- **Line**: 86-89\n- **Severity**: critical\n- **Description**: `ClearCache()` clears `_memberInfoCache` but leaves `_displayNameCache` intact. During hot reload, metadata changes won't be reflected if stale cached display names persist. Both caches need clearing.\n- **Fix**: Clear `_displayNameCache` alongside `_memberInfoCache` in the `ClearCache()` method\n\nNow generating the unified diffs for each fix.\n\n### Fix 1: DisplayName.cs line 27\n```diff\n--- a/src/Components/Web/src/Forms/DisplayName.cs\n+++ b/src/Components/Web/src/Forms/DisplayName.cs\n@@ -24,7 +24,7 @@ public class DisplayName<TValue> : IComponent\n     /// <summary>\n     /// Specifies the field for which the display name should be shown.\n     /// </summary>\n     [Parameter, EditorRequired]\n-    public Expression<Func<TValue>>? For { get; set; }\n+    public Expression<Func<TValue>> For { get; set; }\n```\n\n### Fix 2: DisplayName.cs - Add comparison before render\n```diff\n--- a/src/Components/Web/src/Forms/DisplayName.cs\n+++ b/src/Components/Web/src/Forms/DisplayName.cs\n@@ -35,10 +35,16 @@ public class DisplayName<TValue> : IComponent\n     Task IComponent.SetParametersAsync(ParameterView parameters)\n     {\n         parameters.SetParameterProperties(this);\n \n-        var newDisplayName = ExpressionMemberAccessor.GetDisplayName(For);\n-        _displayName = newDisplayName;\n-        _render Handle.Render(BuildRenderTree);\n+        // Only re-render if the For expression has changed\n+        if (_previousFieldAccessor != For)\n+        {\n+            var newDisplayName = ExpressionMemberAccessor.GetDisplayName(For);\n+            _displayName = newDisplayName;\n+            _previousFieldAccessor = For;\n+            _renderHandle.Render(BuildRenderTree);\n+        }\n \n         return Task.CompletedTask;\n     }\n```\n\nThe concern here is that Expression objects use reference equality rather than value equality, so the comparison `_previousFieldAccessor != For` will only detect when a completely different object reference is passed. However, this approach is actually sound for Blazor components—parent components typically pass the same expression object reference unless they're explicitly recreating it, so reference equality serves as a reasonable cache key.\n\nA more direct approach would be to compare the extracted display name itself instead of the expression object. This avoids the complexity of expression comparison and ensures we only re-render when the actual output changes:\n\n```csharp\nvar newDisplayName = ExpressionMemberAccessor.GetDisplayName(For);\nif (_displayName != newDisplayName)\n{\n    _displayName = newDisplayName;\n    _renderHandle.Render(BuildRenderTree);\n}\n```\n\nThis is more robust because it directly checks whether the rendered content would actually differ, without needing to track the expression reference or extract member information separately.\n\nThe revised fix updates `SetParametersAsync` to perform this string comparison before triggering a render. The diff shows adding the conditional check around the assignment and render call. Additionally, clearing both caches in `ExpressionMemberAccessor` ensures stale data doesn't interfere with subsequent lookups.\n\nLooking back at the first fix though, there's an inconsistency worth examining. The `For` parameter is marked with `EditorRequired`, which enforces that users must provide it, yet it's declared as nullable with the `?` operator. In Blazor's parameter validation, `EditorRequired` doesn't actually prevent null at runtime—it's more of a design-time hint for developers.\n\nThe actual usage shows the problem clearly: `ExpressionMemberAccessor.GetDisplayName(For)` calls a method that immediately throws if the argument is null. So the property should be non-nullable to match its actual requirements.\n\nThe bug stems from the type signature being misleading—declaring it nullable when the code demands it be set creates a gap where `For = null` would compile but fail at runtime. The analyzer also expects this to be required in markup, so making it non-nullable aligns the declaration with reality.\n\nLooking deeper at `ExpressionMemberAccessor`, the `GetMemberInfo` method handles different expression types like `UnaryExpression` with `Convert` and `MemberExpression`, throwing descriptive errors for invalid inputs. There are also multiple `GetDisplayName` overloads to consider.\n\nThe first overload takes a `MemberInfo` directly (line 53), while the second accepts an `Expression<Func<TValue>>` (line 79). In `DisplayName.cs`, we're calling the expression-based version with the `For` parameter, which should work correctly given the proper type. These overloads coexist fine through method overloading.\n\nRegarding the Blazor re-rendering behavior: when parameters change, `SetParametersAsync` gets invoked, and components control their own render cycles. Calling `Render` should only happen when explicitly needed, not automatically on parameter updates.\n\nThe key issue is that if a parent passes a different expression object pointing to the same member, we'd unnecessarily re-render identical content. Comparing the new display name against the previous one prevents this waste. The `_previousFieldAccessor` field tracks the prior expression to enable this comparison, ensuring we only trigger renders when the actual display name changes. if the expression object is recreated on each parent render—which happens when using inline lambdas like `@(() => model.PropertyA)`—then reference equality fails. Every render creates a new object, triggering unnecessary re-evaluations even when the underlying property hasn't changed. Instead, comparing the resolved display names would be more reliable: extract the display name and only re-render if it actually differs from the previous value.\n\nThe three key fixes are: removing nullability from the `For` parameter to prevent runtime exceptions, comparing display names rather than expression references to avoid spurious re-renders, and ensuring both caches clear during hot reload to maintain consistency.\n\nReconsidering the severity of the nullable parameter issue—while `EditorRequired` enforces provision at compile time, the nullable type annotation creates a misleading contract. If someone passes a null expression object (whether through error or edge case), it would throw at runtime. The real problem is the contradiction between the nullable type and the required constraint, which violates the nullability contract even if normal usage patterns avoid triggering it.\n\nComparing the two issues: the cache clearing bug actively breaks hot reload functionality, making it critical. The nullable parameter is more of a code smell—it works in practice due to `EditorRequired`, but it's semantically wrong and could cause confusion or runtime failures in edge cases. I'd rate it as high severity but not critical like the cache issue.\n\nThe `ExpressionMemberAccessor.GetDisplayName` method isn't nullable and will throw if passed null, so the property should be non-nullable to catch this at compile time rather than runtime. Looking back through the files, the cache clearing appears to be the most pressing issue to fix first, followed by the type contract violation in the nullable parameter.\n\nReviewing the component implementation, `DisplayName.cs` properly handles the render lifecycle and parameter processing.
